point_001: The theoretical argument is difficult to follow for non-specialists, and the paper lacks sufficient intuitive explanations or paraphrases of the main results to make them accessible to a broader audience.
point_002: The novelty of the theoretical contribution relative to Levine (2020) is unclear; the paper borrows heavily from that prior work (including repeated text), and it is not well articulated what new conclusions the current analysis adds beyond what could already be inferred from that paper.
point_003: The connection between the theoretical analysis and the proposed empirical methods (kNN-TAPT and kNN-pretraining) is weak and incoherent — it does not logically follow from the theoretical results that retrieving semantically similar sentences and placing them in the same training example will improve performance.
point_004: The empirical evaluation is limited in scope; experiments are restricted to sentence similarity tasks and open-domain QA, while other relevant sentence-pair tasks (e.g., MNLI, RTE, and other NLI benchmarks) are not explored.
point_005: The kNN-TAPT results on semantic similarity tasks suffer from a methodological confound: the data retrieval method is based on semantic similarity, and the evaluation tasks are also semantic similarity benchmarks, making it unclear whether the gains generalize to other task types.
point_006: The zero-shot QA results show only marginal absolute improvements (F1 from ~0.001 to ~0.01), suggesting the models tested may be too small or the task too difficult for meaningful conclusions; it is not shown whether larger models succeed at this task or whether an easier task exists where the method provides a clearer advantage.
point_007: The proposed approach of interleaving semantically related but non-contiguous text chunks may disrupt within-document coherence, potentially harming the model's ability to learn natural intra-document dependencies, and could also degrade the quality of generated text.
point_008: The paper does not provide experiments or preliminary results on the 'self-improving' aspect of the technique, which is mentioned multiple times as a promising direction but remains entirely unsupported empirically.
point_009: It is not sufficiently explained or empirically justified why the in-context bias improvement would be useful for tasks like NaturalQuestions, where the connection to cross-document dependency modeling is not intuitive.
